claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 14 years old and does not live with you.
  - Blake is 19 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 14 (under 16)
  - Someone who lives with the child is already claiming
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 19 and in approved education
  - Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you some questions.

Let's start with: **Do you have any children, and if so, how many children are you inquiring about?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I have two children I'd like to ask about - Alex, who's 14, and Blake, who's 19.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! I'll help you determine eligibility for both Alex and Blake.

Let me start by asking about **Blake, who is 19**: Is Blake currently in any approved education or training?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Blake is in approved education - he's studying for his A-levels at sixth form college.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's helpful, thank you! Blake may still be eligible since he's in approved education, even though he's over 16.

Now I need to ask: **Does Blake work for 24 hours or more per week, or does he receive any benefits in his own right** (such as Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Blake doesn't work that much - he just focuses on his studies. And he doesn't get any benefits in his own name either.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent, thank you. Now let me ask about your living situation:

**Do both Alex and Blake live with you in the UK?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I live in the UK, in Manchester. Blake lives with me, but Alex doesn't - Alex lives with my ex-partner.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. Since Alex lives with your ex-partner, I need to ask:

**Do you make any financial contributions towards Alex's upkeep?** This could include things like clothes, presents, food, pocket money, or contributions towards their accommodation. If so, approximately how much do you contribute per week or month?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, I'm not currently contributing financially towards Alex.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for clarifying. One more question about Alex: **Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex** (such as your ex-partner)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, my ex-partner is already claiming Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. I now have enough details to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for both children.

Let me make my determination:

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## Summary of Your Child Benefit Eligibility

**For Alex (age 14): Not Eligible ❌**

Since Alex lives with your ex-partner and you're not making any financial contributions towards Alex's upkeep, you're not considered responsible for Alex under Child Benefit rules. Additionally, only one person can claim Child Benefit for a child, and your ex-partner is already claiming for Alex.

**For Blake (age 19): Eligible ✓**

You are eligible to claim Child Benefit for Blake because:
- Blake lives with you in the UK
- Blake is under 20 and in approved education (A-levels)
- Blake doesn't work 24+ hours per week
- Blake doesn't receive benefits in their own right

You can proceed with claiming Child Benefit for Blake. If you haven't already done so, you can make a claim through the official process. 

**Important note:** If you or your partner have an individual income over £60,000, you may need to pay the High Income Child Benefit tax charge, so it's worth checking that as well.

Is there anything else you'd like to know about your Child Benefit situation?

reasonings: Alex is 14 years old and lives with the claimant's ex-partner, not with the claimant. The claimant is not making any financial contributions towards Alex's upkeep. According to the rules, you'll usually be responsible for a child if you live with them OR if you're paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit towards looking after them. Since neither condition is met, the claimant is not considered responsible for Alex. Additionally, only one person can get Child Benefit for a child, and the ex-partner is already claiming Child Benefit for Alex. Therefore, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

Blake is 19 years old and is in approved education (studying for A-levels at sixth form college). Blake lives with the claimant in the UK. According to the rules, you'll normally still qualify for Child Benefit if your child is under 20 and they stay in approved education or training. Blake does not work for 24 hours or more per week and does not receive any benefits in their own right (such as Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit), which would disqualify them. Blake meets all the eligibility criteria, so the claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for Blake.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has two children: Alex (14) and Blake (19). Alex lives with the claimant's ex-partner who is already claiming Child Benefit for Alex, and the claimant makes no financial contributions towards Alex's upkeep, so they are not eligible for Alex. Blake lives with the claimant, is 19 and in approved education (A-levels), does not work 24+ hours per week, and does not receive benefits in their own right, so the claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for Blake.
